guest: Andrew Wilkinson, founder Tiny (holding company for internet businesses)

business_ideas.idea: Digital job board marketplace with SEO moat and recurring subscription revenue
Modern investment bank/financial advisory for bootstrap founders (advisory on debt, M&A, secondaries)
Hyper-targeted personal advertising/awareness service (appearing in one decision-maker's entire information diet)
Direct-to-consumer SodaStream competitor with subscription model and better DTC execution
Premium coffee brewing device scaled online (AeroPress acquisition)
Designer cat furniture e-commerce store (HJ Muse)
Project management SaaS competing with Asana (Flow)

predictions.text: Crypto and FTX collapse will have lasting negative effect on trust and institutional adoption
Most valuable businesses will be verb-status products with multi-decade staying power (Kleenex model)

dynamics: Andrew drives through extended founder stories and business postmortems; Sam and Shaan react with fascination to both successes and brutal failures. Tone is educational and confessional—Andrew openly admits losses and wrong turns. Shifts between storytelling (Pixel Union, Famous Original failures) to ranking frameworks (business difficulty) to pitching new venture (Tenzing). High respect for founder wisdom through experience.
frameworks: Business Difficulty Ranking — Hardest: brick-and-mortar + physical goods + labor → Medium: agencies/consulting → Easiest: digital subscriptions/job boards
Warren Buffett's Operational Involvement Model — active management of details (See's Candy letter) vs. passive capital allocation myth
Verb Status = Market Dominance — products that become verbs (Google, Uber, AeroPress) signal defensible market position
Founder Dilemma: Hold Forever vs. Sell — usually better to hold through downturns because businesses rarely die instantly
Wrong Business Before Right Business — pattern of trying hard business first teaches what not to do
Spreadsheet Investors vs. Operational Investors — understanding actual business dynamics vs. financial metrics alone
learnings: E-commerce and physical retail have razor-thin margins (2-3%) vs. software (30-50% EBITDA); capital tied up in inventory compounds difficulty
Restaurants and brick-and-mortar operations are unforgiving due to coordination complexity and labor dependency; early success in hard business breeds overconfidence
Operational expertise from one business transfers better to acquisitions than capital alone; understanding what works in practice beats spreadsheet analysis
Missing government incentives (R&D tax credits) or financial tools (credit lines, secondaries) costs founders millions in unrealized value

banger_quotes.quote: I'm a better businessman because I'm an investor; better investor because I'm a businessman
If someone bails, the deep fryer's broken—it's night and day vs. internet businesses
Fiji fighting the United States with unlimited budget—it's ridiculous
99% is in the telling about it, 1% is in the drinking
Better to hold through downswings than sell at 1x profit when dying

numbers_dropped: Pixel Union at reacquisition: $4 million annual profit
HJ Muse (cat furniture): $300,000 invested, total loss
Mealime sale to Albertsons: tens of millions
SodaStream US growth (by hired CEO): to $200 million
Canadian R&D tax credits missed: $1 million
AeroPress retail penetration: 95% of gourmet coffee shops globally
Vungle (friend Jack Smith): $800 million sale at age 29
Vista Equity Partners (SaaS PE): $100+ billion in revenue
notable: Rare founder postmortem of both successes and catastrophic failures across wildly different business models; Andrew's journey from 'trying everything' to disciplined acquirer mirrors Buffett's evolution. Introduces Tenzing (investment banking for bootstrapped founders) as market gap solution.
